lesson.menu_desc: Hold the renewal meeting and report what each side said -- run the agenda, take a vote on the new terms, and report decisions with reported speech (the director said the deal was final; they told us they would extend) -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- Negotiation V -- the renewal meeting: running it and reporting what each side said with reported speech
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Nilo Mesquita Patussi | Aula 18 -- Negotiation V

listenings.text: Hello again, Doctor Nilo, Sofia here from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. Last time you managed the relationship: you handled a default, you sent a notice, and you protected the goodwill. The result was wonderful, the sponsor wants to renew. So now you must run the renewal meeting, a formal meeting where both sides agree the new terms and vote on them. Today you learn two things, how to run that meeting, and how to report what people said in it. So here is some advice. First, every formal meeting follows an agenda. You open with a short summary of why you are meeting. You check who is present, the attendees, and you read the apologies, the names of the people who could not come. Then you go through each point, and for each one you restate the proposal clearly, you outline the options, and you put it to a vote. Second, a vote has its own language. One person makes a motion, that is a formal proposal. Another person seconds it, that means they support it so the vote can happen. Then everyone votes. Some vote yes, some vote no, and some abstain, that means they choose not to vote at all. If everyone agrees, the decision is unanimous. When the vote passes, you have a resolution, an official decision of the meeting. Finally, you ratify the new contract, that means you formally approve it, and you adjourn the meeting, that means you close it. Now the second skill, reporting. After the meeting, you must tell others what was said, and for that you use reported speech. When you report, the verb usually steps back one tense. Present becomes past. So, if the director says the deal is final, later you report, the director said the deal was final. Will becomes would. If they say we will extend the term, you report, they told us they would extend the term. And remember the difference between say and tell. You say something, but you tell someone. So you say the price is fair, but you tell the board the price is fair. Run the meeting with order, Doctor, and report it with care, and your renewal will be clear to everyone. Good luck.

Let me tell you how the renewal meeting for our biggest sponsor went at Corinthians. It was a formal meeting in the boardroom, and I was the chair, so I had to run it and then report it to the board. I opened with a short summary. I said the purpose was to approve the renewal for two more seasons. I checked the attendees, six people were present, and I read the apologies, two directors could not come. Then I went through the agenda. For the first point, the new yearly fee, I restated the proposal and outlined two options. The marketing director made a motion to accept the higher fee. Another director seconded it. We voted. Four people voted yes, nobody voted no, and one person chose to abstain. So the resolution passed. For the second point, the exclusivity, I outlined the extension to four seasons. This time the vote was unanimous, everyone agreed. After we ratified the new contract, I adjourned the meeting. The hard part came the next morning, when I had to report everything to the president, who was not there. I used reported speech. I told him the board had approved the renewal. I said the marketing director had made a motion, and that the others had seconded it. I told him the vote on the fee was four to zero, with one abstention, and that the vote on the exclusivity was unanimous. He asked me what the directors had said about the price. I reported that they had said the new fee was fair, and that they had told me they would sign that week. The president was pleased. The lesson is simple. Running a meeting needs order, an agenda, a motion, a second, a vote, a resolution. But reporting it needs care. Say what was said, step the tense back, and never confuse say and tell. Do both well, and a renewal becomes a clear, official decision that everyone trusts.
